TextENGNew: ...Honestly.
Sipping canned coffee to keep myself awake, I drove through the late-night city, past buildings lit dimly by streetlamps, heading toward the entertainment district.
(Today I was supposed to go back to the dorm and deal with the laundry piling up...)
changeReason: New translation correctly identifies 寮 as 'dorm,' while original uses generic 'home,' missing that the character lives in a dormitory.

TextENGNew: Running the washing machine in the middle of the night was inconsiderate to the neighbors, but my supply of clean clothes was getting dire.
No matter how demanding the job and how little time I had to come home, neglecting to keep oneself presentable was a mark against one's character — at least for a woman.
With that in mind, I had wrapped up my work quickly today, and just as I was checking the last train schedule on my way out, of all the luck — a call came in from the bar where Maeno-san was.
The proprietor said, 'He's passed out drunk flat on the floor, please come pick him up.'
"Drinking yourself under the table at your age... honestly."
Although — since it was apparently Maeno-san himself who specifically asked for me by name, I suppose in situations like this I should take it as a sign of trust and feel a little flattered.
...Or should I simply tell him to stop relying on me?
Genuinely difficult to know.
While I was mulling this over, the sign of the bar I'd been given directions to appeared before me.
``...Oh! Inspector Minai! Thanks for the pickup — much obliged!''
changeReason: New translation corrects the rank from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector' (matching JPN 警部), and also drops the honorific どの.

TextENGNew: "It's not 'much obliged,' what do you think you're doing..."
changeReason: New translation directly addresses the 'ご苦労である' phrase from the previous line, while original paraphrases loosely as 'is that really the first thing out of your mouth.'

TextENGNew: I pulled over to the side of the road and got out, only to be hailed in a hearty voice by Maeno-san, who had collapsed in front of the bar's entrance. I jogged over to him with an expression of unmitigated exasperation.
...His jacket was on the ground, crumpled to a sorry state.
No tie, and his shirt was wrinkled beyond repair.
Every inch the classic hopeless drunk — and at the thought of having to drive him all the way home, I felt like putting my head in my hands in quiet despair.
"Heyyy... you went out of your way to come pick me up this late, I'm deeply grateful! My master, it's almost time to return home!"
"Stop talking nonsense and get up. If you plant yourself here and refuse to move, you're obstructing the bar's business."
"Oh! If you think you can haul me in, be my guest! I'll tell you right now, dragging a police officer off in public is going to embarrass you more than me!"
...No, I'm quite certain the embarrassment there would be yours.
I kept that retort to myself, and for the moment let it slide with a '...yes, yes.'
Getting into a proper back-and-forth with a drunk would only invite odd accusations and wear me down.
"Now listen here... do you have any idea who I am? I'm a Senior Superintendent, you know — a rank that makes even children stop crying!"
changeReason: New translation correctly identifies the rank as 'Senior Superintendent' (警視正) and preserves the idiom 泣く子も黙る (makes even children stop crying), while original uses the lower rank 'superintendent' and replaces the idiom with 'intimidating.'

TextENGNew: "Yes, I am well aware how important you are, which is why I'd ask you to refrain from making any further statements that would diminish the dignity of the police force. If the press saw you like this—"
"Pfft...! You think being afraid of the press is any way to run a police force?!"
(This is hopeless. I can't do a thing with him.)
I actually considered leaving him here and heading home, but given future working arrangements that wasn't really an option, and I breathed out a deep sigh.
Still — these past weeks working under him in the Public Relations Division, I had been developing genuine respect for Maeno-san, and now, in this one moment, it had taken a nosedive.
Maybe I should start refusing unpaid overtime for a while...
changeReason: New translation specifies 'unpaid overtime' (matching JPN サービス残業, a specific term for uncompensated work), while original just says 'overtime.'

TextENGNew: "What on earth possessed you to drink this much...? You were always boasting about how you were used to entertaining clients and could hold your liquor."
"Now, now, Inspector. Please give him a break, just for tonight."
At that, the proprietor — who had apparently been looking after Maeno-san up until a moment ago — stepped in with a wry smile, attempting to soothe me.
changeReason: New translation merges content from the following entry into this one, describing the proprietor stepping in, while the original keeps only the brief 'After saying that.'

TextENGNew: The old man who had been looking after Maeno-san until now, apparently the shopkeeper, cut in with a bitter smile on his face and tried to calm me down.
changeReason: Reverted: shifted: TextENGNew says "on close terms" when JPN describes shopkeeper cutting in with a smile

TextENGNew: Judging by the way he spoke, he and Maeno-san were well acquainted.
"Just for tonight — but—"
"...It's been a very long time since Maeno-san drank like this. It tends to happen when something truly awful has occurred."
"...Something awful? Oh—"
That made me pause and realize.
...He was referring to the disciplinary hearing, held in response to a recent report from an investigative committee on a certain case.
Two years ago.
...A young boy had been abducted on his way home by an unknown perpetrator.
That night, the criminal called the boy's parents by the book, demanding a ransom.
The police, contacted by the family, requested a press embargo under a covert investigation directive, and poured every effort into capturing the perpetrator and searching for the hostage.
But... every lead hit a dead end, and on top of that, the perpetrator vanished immediately after the ransom was exchanged.
The boy's whereabouts became completely unknown, and the investigation fell entirely cold...
Then... just recently, two full years after the incident.

A man arrested for a minor crime confessed in the course of conversation that he had carried out the abduction — and disclosed that he had killed the boy immediately after fleeing with the ransom, burying him in a temple cemetery in the city.
changeReason: New translation renders ふとした会話の流れで as 'in the course of conversation' (casual/incidental), while original says 'during questioning' (formal interrogation), changing how the confession came about.

TextENGNew: Investigators who went to the scene based on his testimony found a skeletal body in the grounds, exactly as he had described.
The two-year case came to the worst possible conclusion.
(It couldn't be helped that the police were blamed for taking so long to catch the perpetrator. But what became the issue was the timing of the press embargo—)

After that, in order to identify the reasons the kidnapping-murder investigation had ended in failure, the Personnel Division of the National Police Agency spearheaded the formation of an investigative committee.
Their report revealed that in the city where the perpetrator had first fled to after his escape, residents had been reporting a suspicious individual to local authorities at a relatively early stage.

In other words, had the information not been suppressed by the press embargo — or had the embargo been lifted quickly — it was pointed out that not only could the perpetrator have been apprehended, but the hostage's life might have been saved.
And so the media, united in its criticism, condemned the police's investigative posture, arguing that the failure to catch the perpetrator was a result of misjudging when to impose the press embargo.

Within the police as well, there was pressure to hold accountable whoever had issued the directive — and Maeno-san, as the officer who had submitted the press embargo approval to the Chief of Secretariat, received a censure at the disciplinary hearing just days ago...
changeReason: Old uses 'singled out' (vague) for けん責 (formal censure) and 'chief cabinet secretary' for 官房長 (Chief of Secretariat) — wrong title and imprecise punishment, but the core event of Maeno being disciplined is preserved.

TextENGNew: Maeno-san had probably sealed off the information early to put the hostage's life first, and had delayed lifting the embargo so as not to drive the perpetrator into a psychological corner after the ransom was exchanged.
...And yet, since the worst outcome had occurred, those judgments were now being condemned as mistakes.
Thinking of that injustice and that absurdity... I felt hollow.
(He was pretending not to be affected, but... it really hurt him.)
Of course, not because his own evaluation had dropped.
...That wasn't the kind of person he was — I had at least come to understand that in a month's worth of working together.
The reason he had been brought low enough to need the help of alcohol was probably because he was thinking of how the victim's family must feel...
...Maeno-san. Time to go home. Can you stand?
I called out to him, and then — with help from the proprietor — guided Maeno-san's arm behind my neck and propped him up so I could support him.
changeReason: New translation correctly describes putting Maeno-san's arm around her neck to support him, while original incorrectly says she 'tossed my arm behind Maeno-san's back.'

TextENGNew: I opened the passenger door, and with both of us working together, got him settled into the seat, then picked up his jacket from the ground and brushed off the dirt that had gotten onto it.
His inside pocket still had not only his wallet, but his police badge in it.
changeReason: Old says 'Rather than a wallet' implying only the badge was found; JPN 財布どころか means both wallet and badge were present — small factual difference about items found on the body.

TextENGNew: ...It was something of a miracle no one had stolen it.
Something tucked into the police badge caught my eye, and I looked at it in surprise.
In the photograph was a young detective who appeared to be Maeno-san in his earlier years.
And beside him—
"Father...?"
There was no way I could mistake my own father.
The person standing next to young Maeno-san was Father — Yuusuke Minai himself.
After expressing my heartfelt thanks to the proprietor, I started the car.
The national highway through the metropolitan area, clogged bumper to bumper during the day, was comparatively sparse at this hour.
At this rate, I could reach Maeno-san's home before daybreak.
Gripping the steering wheel, navigating the signals and oncoming traffic, I glanced briefly at the passenger seat.
Maeno-san was sunk deep into the seat, fast asleep and snoring.
His glasses looked like they might fall off and get lost if left alone, so I removed them from his face and tucked them into his breast pocket.

changeReason: New translation drops 'やっぱり' (as I thought/so that's it), reducing the line to just an ellipsis and losing the character's moment of realization.

TextENGNew: Seeing his face without the glasses, I felt something click in my memory.
Because of those thick-framed glasses, I hadn't realized until now — but I had a feeling I had met this man before I became a police officer.
But... where?
Where had I met Maeno-san...?
...That's right, I remember.
The young detective who sometimes accompanied Father home when he came in drunk in the dead of night.
Father was older, and yet for some reason Father always spoke to this person with deference — I always thought it was strange.
But Mother had told us not to ask too much about Father's police work at home, so we had never known why.
The answer to that... today, at last... I think I understand.
Maeno-san murmured quietly, in the midst of his sleeping breath.
Needless to say, there was no reason for Maeno-san — who was older and outranked me — to use 'san' when speaking of me.
Which meant that 'Minai-san' he was referring to was without a doubt...
"It's all right... Minai-san... She's your daughter... I'll make something of her — I promise..."
"...Maeno-san."
My eyes grew hot, and I blinked over and over.
The neon signs of the city streaming in through the windshield seemed suddenly to blur into smeared streaks of color...
